chapterSummary: This chapter delves into Gatsby's true origins as James Gatz, revealing his self-invention and his formative years with Dan Cody, which shaped his ambition and wealth. Later, Tom and Daisy Buchanan attend one of Gatsby's parties, where Daisy is unimpressed by the West Egg crowd, leading to Gatsby's profound disappointment and his desperate desire to recreate the past.

characters.name: Nick Carraway
characters.description: Observes Gatsby's past and present, and reflects on the social dynamics of West Egg.

knowledge.content: Gatsby's true name is James Gatz, and he invented the persona of Jay Gatsby at age seventeen.
Gatsby's parents were 'shiftless and unsuccessful farm people,' a background he rejected.
Gatsby's early life as a clam-digger and salmon-fisher, and his brief, dismaying time at St. Olaf's College.
The full story of Gatsby's five-year mentorship with Dan Cody, including Cody's background, their travels, and Cody's death.
Gatsby was supposed to inherit $25,000 from Cody, but it was legally diverted to Ella Kaye.
Tom Buchanan's suspicion that Gatsby is a bootlegger.
Daisy's discomfort and disapproval of the West Egg party guests, despite her initial excitement.
Gatsby's profound disappointment that Daisy didn't enjoy his party and his desperate desire to recreate the past with her.
Gatsby's belief that Daisy should tell Tom she never loved him, and his plan to marry her from her Louisville home as if five years hadn't passed.
His own belief that 'you can't repeat the past,' which Gatsby vehemently denies.
Gatsby's memory of kissing Daisy five years prior, and the profound, almost spiritual significance of that moment for him.

characters.name: Jay Gatsby
characters.description: His true origins as James Gatz are revealed, and he struggles with Daisy's reaction to his party and his desire to reclaim the past.

knowledge.content: Tom Buchanan's presence at his house makes him uneasy.
Tom Buchanan does not remember him from their previous brief encounter.
The woman in the brown riding-habit (Mr. Sloane's companion) is interested in him.
Tom Buchanan is perturbed by Daisy's presence at his party.
Daisy did not enjoy his party and felt offended by the West Egg crowd.
His desperate desire for Daisy to tell Tom she never loved him and to return to Louisville to marry him as if no time had passed.
His belief that 'of course you can' repeat the past.

characters.name: Dan Cody
characters.description: A pioneer debauchee and Gatsby's mentor, whose wealth and lifestyle profoundly influenced young James Gatz.

characters.name: Ella Kaye
characters.description: A newspaper woman who became Dan Cody's mistress and inherited his fortune.

characters.name: Reporter
characters.description: An ambitious young reporter from New York seeking a statement from Gatsby.

knowledge.content: He heard Gatsby's name in connection with something newsworthy, though he doesn't fully understand it.

characters.name: James Gatz
characters.description: Gatsby's original identity before he reinvented himself.

characters.name: Gatz's Parents
characters.description: Shiftless and unsuccessful farm people, rejected by their son's imagination.

characters.name: Mr. Sloane
characters.description: An acquaintance of Tom Buchanan, haughty and dismissive.

knowledge.content: Gatsby is trying to impress him and his companion.

characters.name: Woman in Brown Riding-Habit
characters.description: A pretty woman accompanying Tom and Mr. Sloane, initially reserved but later cordial and flirtatious.

knowledge.content: Gatsby is interested in her.

characters.name: Tom Buchanan
characters.description: Attends Gatsby's party with Daisy, expressing suspicion and disapproval of Gatsby and his guests.

knowledge.content: Gatsby is trying to impress him.
He does not remember Gatsby from their previous encounter.
He is perturbed by Daisy 'running around alone' and her presence at Gatsby's party.
His belief that 'women run around too much these days' and meet 'all kinds of crazy fish'.
He doesn't know anyone at Gatsby's party and finds the crowd uninteresting.
He is suspicious that Gatsby is a bootlegger.
His intention to find out who Gatsby really is and what he does.

characters.name: Daisy Buchanan
characters.description: Initially excited by Gatsby's party, but ultimately offended by the West Egg crowd, leading to Gatsby's disappointment.

knowledge.content: She finds the atmosphere of Gatsby's parties exciting and flirtatious.
She recognizes a 'gorgeous, scarcely human orchid of a woman' (a movie celebrity) at the party.
She likes the 'man with the sort of blue nose' (a small producer).
She finds the West Egg party guests 'common but pretty' and is offended by their 'raw vigour'.
She believes many guests are uninvited and Gatsby is too polite to object.
She claims Gatsby owned 'drugstores, a lot of drugstores' to deflect Tom's suspicion.

characters.name: Movie Star
characters.description: A celebrity at Gatsby's party, described as a 'gorgeous, scarcely human orchid of a woman'.

characters.name: Movie Director
characters.description: The director of the Movie Star, seen bending over her at Gatsby's party.

characters.name: Small Producer
characters.description: A man with a 'blue nose' whom Daisy liked at Gatsby's party.

characters.name: Miss Baedeker
characters.description: A tipsy guest at Gatsby's party who gets her head stuck in a pool.

characters.name: Doctor Civet
characters.description: A guest at Gatsby's party with shaky hands, who suggests Miss Baedeker leave liquor alone.

characters.name: Massive and Lethargic Woman
characters.description: A guest at Gatsby's party who defends Miss Baedeker and urges Daisy to play golf.

locations.name: Gatsby's Mansion
locations.description: The site of Gatsby's extravagant parties, now also a place where his past is revealed and his present hopes are tested.
lore: A place where contemporary legends about Gatsby, such as an 'underground pipeline to Canada' or living in a boat, circulate.
The setting for Gatsby's parties, which Daisy finds both exciting and offensive due to the 'raw vigour' of the West Egg crowd.

locations.name: Lake Superior
locations.description: A large freshwater lake in North America, where James Gatz first encountered Dan Cody.
lore: The location where James Gatz, a clam-digger and salmon-fisher, reinvented himself as Jay Gatsby upon meeting Dan Cody.

locations.name: Tuolomee
locations.description: Dan Cody's yacht, a symbol of wealth and glamour to young James Gatz.
lore: The vessel on which Gatsby spent five years as Cody's steward, mate, skipper, secretary, and jailor, traveling around the Continent.

locations.name: St. Olaf's College
locations.description: A small Lutheran college in southern Minnesota, where Gatsby briefly attended.
lore: Gatsby stayed for two weeks, dismayed by its 'ferocious indifference to the drums of his destiny'.

locations.name: Little Girl Bay
locations.description: The specific location on Lake Superior where Dan Cody's yacht dropped anchor and he met James Gatz.

locations.name: Duluth
locations.description: A city in Minnesota where Dan Cody took young Gatsby to buy him clothes.

locations.name: Boston
locations.description: A city where Ella Kaye came aboard Dan Cody's yacht, shortly before his death.

locations.name: Nick's House
locations.description: A small bungalow, serving as a brief refuge for Gatsby and Daisy during the party.

threads.description: Nick's disillusionment with the East and its moral landscape
threads.development: Nick's disillusionment deepens as he observes the superficiality and moral emptiness of Gatsby's party through Daisy's eyes, and reflects on the 'raw vigour' of West Egg.

threads.description: The mystery surrounding Jay Gatsby and his true nature
threads.development: Gatsby's true origins as James Gatz and his formative years with Dan Cody are revealed, clarifying the source of his ambition and wealth, and explaining his self-invention. However, his desperate desire to repeat the past introduces a new layer of complexity to his character.

threads.description: The crumbling marriage and moral decay of Tom and Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: Tom's suspicion and disapproval of Gatsby and his guests, coupled with Daisy's flirtatious behavior and her eventual offense at the West Egg crowd, highlight the ongoing tensions and moral decay within their marriage.

threads.description: The social and moral contrast between East Egg and West Egg
threads.development: The contrast is sharply drawn as Daisy, an East Egg resident, is offended by the 'raw vigour' and lack of social grace among the West Egg partygoers, reinforcing the perceived moral and social divide.

threads.description: The destructive nature of Tom Buchanan's affair with Myrtle Wilson
threads.development: This thread is dormant in this chapter, but Tom's general suspicion of 'newly rich people' and his possessiveness over Daisy hint at his underlying character flaws that drive his affair.

threads.description: Nick's evolving relationship and moral judgment of Jordan Baker
threads.development: Nick's association with Jordan is mentioned as he tries to 'ingratiate himself with her senile aunt,' indicating a continued, if background, development of their relationship.

threads.description: Gatsby's singular romantic obsession with Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: Gatsby's profound disappointment over Daisy's reaction to his party and his desperate, almost delusional, desire to recreate the past with her intensifies this obsession, revealing its all-consuming and potentially destructive nature.

scenes.summary: A reporter from New York arrives at Gatsby's door, seeking a statement about the rumors surrounding him. Gatsby politely deflects, and Nick reflects on the myriad legends that have attached themselves to Gatsby. This encounter prompts Nick to reveal Gatsby's true origins as James Gatz, a farm boy from North Dakota who reinvented himself.

scenes.summary: Nick visits Gatsby, only for Tom Buchanan, Mr. Sloane, and a woman in a brown riding-habit to arrive on horseback. Gatsby, visibly uneasy, tries to be hospitable and engage Tom, who barely remembers him. The woman flirts with Gatsby and invites him to supper, but Mr. Sloane pointedly prevents her from extending the invitation to Nick, and they leave abruptly, leaving Gatsby disappointed.

scenes.summary: Tom and Daisy attend Gatsby's party, which Nick observes through Daisy's eyes, noting her initial excitement but eventual discomfort with the West Egg crowd. Tom, meanwhile, is openly suspicious of Gatsby and his guests, believing them to be bootleggers. Daisy attempts to deflect Tom's inquiries, but the evening leaves Gatsby deeply disappointed by Daisy's reaction.

scenes.summary: After the party, Gatsby confesses to Nick his profound disappointment that Daisy didn't enjoy herself. He reveals his desperate desire for Daisy to tell Tom she never loved him, and his plan to return to Louisville to marry her as if five years hadn't passed. Nick cautions him that 'you can't repeat the past,' but Gatsby vehemently denies it, clinging to his vision of recreating their shared history.
